glossary.definition: A measure of how alike two data objects are; depends on the feature types and distance metrics used.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity was considered in detail in the second topic of the module in regard to the kNN algorithm. It is also used in unsupervised machine learning.

glossary.definition: The average of the current set of feature vectors within a cluster; the center of a cluster around which data objects are grouped based on their distance to the centroid.
glossary.sentence: It is the average of the current set of feature vectors within the cluster (Jones, 2009).

glossary.definition: An unsupervised machine learning algorithm that aims to minimize the sum of distances between data objects and their assigned cluster centroids.
glossary.sentence: The K-Means algorithm is one of the popular algorithms of unsupervised machine learning. According to (Jones, 2009), “the algorithm is popular primarily because it works relatively well and is extremely simple both to understand and to implement” .

glossary.definition: a process where a selected pair of clusters are recursively merged into a single cluster until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level.
glossary.sentence: Figure 2 represents the process of agglomerative hierarchical clustering in which clusters having the smallest distance between them are merged at each step of clustering.

glossary.definition: a top-down clustering approach that starts with a cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters until each data object makes a cluster.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: A specific group of data objects.
glossary.sentence: The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.

glossary.definition: methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Complete-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated.
glossary.sentence: Single-linkage : in deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster.
glossary.sentence: Average-linkage: the distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: a tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset.

glossary.definition: represents merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height.

glossary.definition: terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: a horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A horizontal cut- off line is usually made through the dendrogram to decide the number of clusters based on the hierarchical clustering algorithm.

glossary.definition: metrics evaluating the algorithm's performance with the different number of clusters.

glossary.definition: an ordered sequence of cluster merges.

glossary.definition: demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value.
glossary.sentence: In general, the regression task demands making predictions in a continuous range. Thus, its output is a real value.

glossary.definition: measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.
glossary.sentence: The Mean squared error (MSE) measures the average of the squared prediction errors for individual data objects. It provides positive non-zero values.

glossary.definition: applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.
glossary.sentence: Root mean square error (RMSE) applies a square root to MSE and therefore provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: is the average of the sums of the absolute difference between predicted output values and actual output values. It also provides an error measure in the same unit as the target variable.

glossary.definition: also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: R2 coefficient is also known as the coefficient of determination. It indicates how close the model built (the line/plane found by the regression algorithm) fits the actual data objects available in the dataset.

glossary.definition: represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model.
glossary.sentence: It represents to what extent the observed variance in the actual output values is explained by the features of the constructed model, for example, if the R2 coefficient of a model is 0.88, then approximately 88% of the observed variation in the actual output values can be explained by the model’s features.

glossary.definition: A task where the goal is to assign data objects to predefined classes.
glossary.sentence: Precision is the probability that the classifier is right when labelling an example as positive (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. glossary.sentence: Multiclass classification is when there are more than two classes to which data objects can belong. Multiclass classification is an extension of binary classification; thus, usually, to evaluate performance in multiclass classification, several averaging techniques across classes are used, for example, macro or weighted averaging.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1).
glossary.sentence: True positives (TP) – a data object is positive (label 1), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 1). For example, an e-mail is classified as spam, and it is spam.

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0).
glossary.sentence: True negatives (TN) - a data object is negative (label 0), and the algorithm correctly recognises it as such (label 0). For example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, and it is non-spam.

glossary.definition: a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.
glossary.sentence: False positives (FP) – a data object is negative (label 0), but the algorithm labels it as positive (label 1); for example, an e-mail is classified as spam, but it is not. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as positive.

glossary.definition: a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.
glossary.sentence: False negatives (FN) – a data object is positive (label 1), but the classifier labels it as negative (label 0), for example, an e-mail is classified as non-spam, but it is spam. In this case, we can say that a data object is “falsely” predicted as negative.

glossary.definition: a two-dimensional table of counts of how often each class/category is classified or misclassified as each other category. Thus, it represents detailed information on the number of correct and erroneous predictions for each class/category.

glossary.definition: The number of layers, the number of processing units, the function for combining input signals, and the activation function to be used.

glossary.definition: A metric that evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects.
glossary.sentence: A recall metric (also called sensitivity and true positive rate) evaluates a fraction of data objects classified as positives out of the total actual positive data objects. glossary.definition: A data object that is correctly classified as positive.

glossary.sentence: A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron.

glossary.definition: A synapse that promotes firing in the receiving neuron and has a positive weight.

glossary.definition: A synapse that inhibits firing in the receiving neuron and has a negative weight.
glossary.sentence: Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively.

glossary.sentence: A feed-forward network has connections only in one direction . Every processing unit receives input from 'upstream' processing units and delivers output to 'downstream' processing units; there are no loops. Thus, a feed-forward network represents a function of its current input.

glossary.definition: If he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain. glossary.definition: An automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. glossary.definition: Forming high-level conclusions from collections of raw data.

glossary.definition: Devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.
glossary.sentence: Planning - devising a sequence of actions that will achieve a set of goals given certain starting conditions and run-time constraints.

glossary.definition: Governing the behaviour of a complex environment.

glossary.definition: Represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.

glossary.definition: Schemas that use a graph to represent knowledge, where each node represents a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts, and relations are represented by referencing principles rather than explicit arcs.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge. However, comparing to the networked knowledge representation schemas, each node in the graph represents not a single concept/object, but a complex structure describing an object/concept together with its properties and parts. Moreover, relations are not represented explicitly by arcs at it was done in semantic networks, but a referencing principle is used when one node references another node by its name. Frames are an example of structured knowledge representation schemas.

glossary.definition: Theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.
glossary.sentence: associationist theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts.

glossary.sentence: Demons are usually defined in class and sub-class frames and then inherited and executed in instance frames.

glossary.definition: The selection of one rule from the conflict set.

glossary.definition: The expert system has the goal (a hypothetical solution), and the inference engine attempts to find the evidence to prove it.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base;
